segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]
UNA KRAVETS: Hi, everybody.
Thank you for coming.
Welcome to What's New in Web UI.

I'm Una, a Chrome Developer
Relations engineer

focused on all things
visual on the web platform,

so that means CSS,
HTML, and JavaScript,

that helps you build beautiful,
engaging web interfaces.

We've come a long way
in the past five years.

Incredibly powerful
features that we didn't even

know we could have
in the browser

have not only landed,
but have also gained

cross-browser compatibility
at a faster pace
than they ever have before.

The HasParent selector,
container queries,

color functions,
view transitions,

nesting, trigonometric
functions,

scroll driven animations,
it's truly amazing

what you can now do with
native HTML and CSS.

How lucky are that
we get to experience

this shift, this
golden age of web

UI, this really cool era where
we get to be web developers?

That being said, there are still
some really common UI patterns,

components that we use and
build every day, that are still

really, really
hard to get right,

and as a developer
community, we've

relied heavily on JavaScript
frameworks or complex CSS

tricks, and sometimes
mountains of custom code

to create these UI patterns
that feel like they
should be a lot simpler.

And that's precisely
where the Chrome team

is focusing its efforts,
making those surprisingly

complex, but really
common, UI patterns that

are fundamental in the platform
straightforward to implement.

Today, we're going
to zero in on three

of those notoriously tricky
patterns, the endlessly

frustrating customizable
select, the deceptively

complex carousel, and the
interactive hover card.

These components may
look simple to a user,

but us developers
know the strife

of trying to get the
accessibility right

in the carousel buttons, or
needing to completely rebuild

a select with divs just to
change the font or the colors.

And don't even get
me started on trying

to create these hover
cards that you see
all over the place in web UIs.

The good news is
that the Chrome team

has been working with other
browser vendors, the web

community, and standards bodies
like the CSS working group

and What Working Group, along
with community groups like Open

UI, to standardize solutions
to deliver powerful declarative

APIs that tackle these
challenges head on.

So there is good news
there, and in this talk,

I'm going to explore how
upcoming browser features make

building these patterns a lot
simpler, more performance,
and more accessible.
And these features are landing.

Everything that I'm going
to talk about here today,

with the exception of
one feature at the end,

has shipped in at least
one stable browser engine.

And I also want to note that,
while it may not look complete

yet, those features that
have landed in Chrome

are also a part of a
process called Standards,

and we hope that they'll
also ship across browsers.

So we'll be using baseline
terminology and symbols

to help you understand feature
availability, so look out
for these throughout the talk.

And also, if you didn't
catch What's New in Web

yesterday, you can
definitely catch

that online to learn more
about baseline and features
that have landed.

So first, let's start
with the Select element.

The Select is essential
for countless forms,

but the internal
structure has historically

been obfuscated by the browser,
effectively shielding it

from consistent and
comprehensive CSS

styling across different
browsers and platforms.

But before we even
get into the Select,

we need to talk about its
building blocks, which are
popover and anchor positioning.

Not only are these features
the underlying tech

behind the customizable
Selects, but because

of their primitive nature,
they can do so much more.

So first, let's talk
about the dropdown list

itself, that floating
box of options.

You need it above
everything else in the UI,

easy to dismiss, and
manage focus when it opens,

and customizable
Select leverages

the super useful popover
API to do just that.

I talked about popover
on this stage last year,

and I'm excited to announce
that popover has now

reached baseline newly available
status this year, so it's
in all browsers.

Really exciting to see it
go from one to all of them
in a year.

So as an example, here, we
have the YouTube notifications
pop over just for a visual.

And a way to create a
popover is fairly simple.
You just need two pieces.

The first is a trigger
element, like this button here,

and the second is the
actual popover itself.

All you need to do is
give the popover an ID

and set the popover
attribute on that popover.

Then you connect the popover
to its invoking button
using popover target like this.

Behind the scenes,
the popover API
isn't just showing an element.

It's managing its
entire lifecycle.

Popover automatically
supports top layer

rendering, so you don't
have to fight with z-index

and stacking context
issues anymore.

It provides a built
in light dismiss

functionality on
auto popovers, so

that when a user clicks outside
of the popover area, it closes.

It automatically
manages focus where

the browser handles tab
navigations for the popover when

it's opened and
correctly returns

tab focus to the invoking
element when it's closed,

ensuring predictable
keyboard navigation

regardless of where the popover
is in the dom structure.

And while you still need to
choose the correct element,

whether that's a dialogue,
or apply the correct ARIA

role to define the
element's semantic purpose,

like a menu or a tooltip, the
underlying interaction model
is handled natively.

These declarative features
have been so well received

and work so well that it
makes it really easy to reach

for a popover, but that
might not always be the best
choice for your component.

For example, when you
have an interaction that

blocks the rest of the page
and requires some sort of user

action, the layered UI element
should really be a modal dialog.

But the dialog element was
built before popover was,

so it lacked some of these neat
features like light dismiss

and a nice declarative
API that can be triggered
without JavaScript, until now.

Now, we have the closedby
attribute landing
a few months ago in Chrome 134.

Users can now use
closedby equals

any to enable light dismiss
directly on modal dialogs.

With closedby equals any,
clicking outside of the dialog

or pressing the Escape
key closes the dialog.

This seemingly small interaction
is such a game changer

for simplifying light
dismiss behavior,

and it's awesome to see this
land for the dialog element.

Another area where
repetitive JavaScript is,

it's connecting the
button click to an action,
like opening that dialog.

Command invokers provide a
declarative HTML solution
similar to popover target.

Here, a button invokes
a command dialog.

This is done by using the
command and commandfor pattern,

you can use the very
straightforward command

showmodal, which is akin to the
JavaScript showmodal function.

Then you target the
dialog by referencing

its ID in the command
for attribute, like so.

You can also use this to
close the dialog using
the close command.

This command commandfor
pattern gives us a cleaner way

to link elements
and actions in HTML,

removing boilerplate JavaScript
for many standard behaviors

like that opening and
closing functionality.

And this can be used on any
element, dialog and beyond.

The browser automatically
manages ARIA details for you,

eliminating the need
for extra JavaScript.

For non-supported browsers,

and all you have to do is import

It's pretty cool.

I've used it, and I've checked
